summary: In a brief tribute to MEDITECH founder Neil Pappalardo, Judy Faulkner reflects on the early mentorship he provided to Epic. She praises MEDITECH's decision to remain a private company, noting that it allowed them to prioritize customer care and technology over short-term financial pressures.

principles.statement: Remain a private company to maintain focus on customers and technology.
principles.rationale: Going public introduces short-term financial pressures that distract a company from its core mission of caring for customers and building good technology.
principles.source_quote: ...they never went public, so they avoid the tyranny of the quarter.

mental_models.name: Tyranny of the Quarter
mental_models.description: The concept that public markets force companies into a short-term mindset, optimizing for 90-day financial reporting cycles rather than long-term value creation.
mental_models.example: Judy Faulkner praises MEDITECH for avoiding this dynamic by staying private, which allowed them to focus on technology and customer care.

signature_quotes.text: ...they never went public, so they avoid the tyranny of the quarter.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing why she holds MEDITECH in high regard and respects their business model.

anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It subjects the company to the 'tyranny of the quarter,' forcing leadership to prioritize short-term financial metrics over long-term customer care and technological focus.
